April 
18 t \99L
Honorable Susan Mullen
counci lmember
city of Iilorro Bay
595 Harbor Street
l,lorro Bay, cA 93442
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. I-91-194
Dear councilmember Mu1len:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice as a
city councilmember for city of Morro Bay concerning your
resions ibi litj,es under the confl iqt-of- interest provisions of the
political 
Reform Act (the rra";rr1.1 since your request concerns
potentj-al future decisions, the circumstances of which are not
cl-ear at this time, $re are providing ygu $rith general guidelines
applicable to the decisions generally.z
OUESTION
May you participate in Morro Bay city council decisions
regardiig- proierty oined by wiLliams Brothers corporation, which
is-the piuintirr 
in a lawsuit against you and the city concerning
the property?
CONCLUS ION
while the circurnstances presented in your letter may create
the appearance of a conflict of interest, currently, you do not
nave l^n economic interest in decisions concernj'ng the property

regutations appear at 2 California code of Regulations . Section
ie5oo, et seo^.- A11 references to regulations are to Title 2'
oivision o ot the California Code of Regulations'
2 rnformal assistance does not provide the requestor.with the
:.*m""ity provided by an opinion br fornal written advice' (section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3) .)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O? . sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 o (916)322'5660




Our Fi■e No。 ■-9■―■94
Page 2
owned by Williams Brothers corporation. Under the Act a conf l-ict
of interest exists only where an official's 
economic interest is
involved in a decision. consequently, You are not prohibited from
participating in decisj-ons concerning the property in question or
Measure A, provided the decisions wil] not affect your incone,
assets or liabil,ities 
by $25o or more.
FACTS
In March 1990, the Morro Bay city council approved a
conditional- use permit sought by willj-ams Brothers corporation for
a shoppj,ng center on 30 acres of property within the city. 
In
Novernber 1990, tleasure H was placed on the bal1ot and adopted by
the voters. Measure H reduced the amount of property approved for
the shopping center to L3 acres. At the same election you were
elected to the Morro Bay city council.
In February 1991, williams Brothers Corporation f il,ed suit
against you, another councilmember and the city with respect to
the effect of l{easure H on their interests. 
You have becone
concerned about potential- conflicts of interest with respect
governmentaL decisions regarding the property in questj-on or
Measure H, should such decisions come before the city council.
ANALYS I S
The Political Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
state of California by initiative 
j.n 1974. The purpose for the
conf l- ict-of- interest provisions of the Act was to ensure that
public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would perform
their duties in an irnpartial manner, free frorn bj.as caused !y
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 810o1(b).)
In furtherance of this purpose, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public officia] 
from making, participating in
making, or olherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
As a city councilmember of the City of Morro
Bay you are a public official 
under the Act. (section 82048.)
co-ns-equently, iou may not participate in any decision v,hich will
have a foreseeable and material financial- effect on your ov'n
economic interests.
Section 87103 sets forth economic interests which are
potentiatly disqualifying financial interests under the Act:
An official 
has a financial interest in a
decision within the rneaning of Section 871-oo if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the publ'ic generally, on the


Our Flle No. I-91-■
94
Page 3
official 
or a member of his or her immediate fanily
or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more.
(b) Any real proPerty in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or nore.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a connercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars (5250) or more in value provided to,
received by or prornised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, Partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty doLlars (5250) or more in value
provided to. reclived by, or promised to the public
officiat 
within 12 nonths prior to the time e,hen
the decision is nade.
An econonic interest becones a disquatifying financial
interest uith resPect to any decision which will have a
foreseeable, material finanLial effect on the economic interest'
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial liketihood that it will occur. certainty is not
;A;i;;.-io""""., 
if an effect is only a mere possibiri-ty' it is
no€ ."utonubly foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) -1 FPPC.ops'
198, copy enciosed. ) commission .eg,fatj'ons provide standards for
aeter:nining whether the foreseeable effect of the decision will be
material, depending on the nature of the decision and economic
interest involved. (Regulation L87 02 .')
Ho$rever. your facts do not reveal any economic interests
involved in future decisions concerning the property in question'
For exaEpIe, your facts do not suggest any investment interests
ir, "i-piiiiiins 
wittr any busines!-entity-that.wiIl 
be-affected bv
fuiure decisions concerning the real property in questron'
(section 87103(a) and (d) .i 
rf the aevelopment oi.th" property in
question $/ouLd result in any business entity in which. Yoy af" u
director, officer. partner, trustee, employle or.in which-you have
an investnent interLst of 51,ooo or rnore losing i-ncome' you may


Our File No. I-9■―■94
Page 4
have a conflict of interest. 
(see e.cr., I{ilson Advice Letter,
A-90-141, copy enclosed.) This does not appear to be the case.
Moreover, ownership of real proPerty near the property
subject to the decision might also create a conflict of interest.
(seation 87103(b); see e.q., BaIl Advice Letter, No. A-89-250,
copy enclosed.) Again, your facts do not reflect property
interests which will be affected by decisions concerning the
williams Brothers Corporation's property-
You have not received income from the owners of the Property
of $25o or more in the past 12 months. (Section 87103(c); webb
Advice Letter. No. I-89-415, copy enclosed.) You stated in your
Ietter that the lawsuit is still 
in the early stages, and as such
any monetary award to the plaintiffs 
is purely speculative-
further, lre presume that the owners of the property have not
provided gifts of S25o or more to you in the past 12 months.
(Section 87103 (e) . )
Direct Effects on Your Assets Income and Liabilities
Ho$/ever, governmental decisions rnay stiIl 
be disqualifying
where the decilion witt result in your personal- expenses, incone,
assets, or liabilities 
increasing or decreasing by at least $250'
(Regulation lA702.1(a) (4) , copy enclosed. )
For example, a decision whj,ch will foreseeably affect
potential darniges against you personally may also be . disqual ifying
tec"r=" you are potentially liabIe for $25o or more in damages.
For examlte, in 6ur advice letter to Donald E. Srnith (Smith Advice
Letter, ilo. A-e7-305, copy enclosed) we advised that a defendant/
public official 
could not participate in decisions to settle the
punitive damages portion oi a lawsuit or in decisions pertaining
to whether the agency should pay the official's 
punitive damages
if the plaintiff 
was successfut in the litigation. 
This was
because the agency had no obligations to pay the putative darnages,
and the agen"!'s decision to pay them $rould alleviate a debt of
the official. 
Thus, you may nol participate in any city council
decisions on the lawsuit.
However, in the same letter we advised that where the agency
was obligated under state law to indemnify the officials for the
defense ind payment of claims and judgenents, the official's 
would
not have a f-inlnciat interest and could participate in the
deliberations. 
trrus, wtrere the lawsuit challenges your conduct in
your official 
capacily you have no financial interest in the
iecision provided the city is obligated to pay the damages'
I'loreover, if the litigation 
with williams Brothers
Corporation became a debt you owed to them for danages, and your
decis j.on on the property hrould reduce the damages by $250 or more'
you could not paititipale. 
(See e.g., 9al!-esgE Advice Letter,
iro. r-89-19s; iee als-o, earbosa e&ice r'etter, A-90-060, copies
"""r"..a. i a"""tai-"g io-your facts no decision has been reached

―■94
Page 5
on the merits of the case. There i.s currently no money o$red to
williams Brothers corporation, and williams Brothers Corporation's
success in the litigation 
is purely speculative at this tine.
However, if a decision is reached in the case requiring you to pay
damages to Wil-Iians Brothers Corporation, you should contact us to
obtain further advice on potential restrictions on your
participation in governmenta 1 decisions.
If you have any further questions regarding this natter,
please feet free to contact ne at (916) 322-5907.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
P I, Legal Division
